explanation: The key distinction is who controls access and when. Tools are invoked by the model on demand — every read is a round trip tool call that consumes context. Resources are published by the server and consumed by the application, which can pre-load relevant ones at session start, cache stable content, and surface it without model-initiated calls. For stable reference content like documentation pages that don't change based on Claude's actions, resources remove the repeated tool-call overhead that is causing the latency and context bloat.

question: You are building an MCP server for your coding standards. You need two capabilities: (1) Claude should programmatically check compliance during its own code analysis, and (2) users should be able to type '/code-review' in Claude Code to trigger a standards review. Which MCP primitives correctly serve these two different interaction patterns?
options: Two tools — tools can serve both model-initiated and user-initiated flows
A tool for (1) and a prompt for (2) — tools are model-callable actions; prompts are reusable templates surfaced to users as slash commands
A resource for (1) so standards are always in context, and a tool for (2)
A single prompt for both — prompts are more flexible and can serve both model and user interactions
explanation: The key distinction is who initiates the interaction. Actions the model takes autonomously during its reasoning → tool (model-controlled). User-invoked workflows surfaced as slash commands → prompt (designed to be composed into conversations by users or client applications). These are different interaction patterns served by different primitives.

question: A developer added a rule to the team's project-root CLAUDE.md: 'Always use async/await, never callbacks.' Her personal ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md says: 'Match the existing code style of the file you are editing.' She is working in a legacy directory that uses callbacks throughout. Claude keeps writing callback-style code. Which rule is it following and why?
options: The team CLAUDE.md rule — it is committed to the repository and takes precedence over personal preferences

The personal ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md rule — user-level files load first and establish the baseline. The 'match existing style' directive points Claude at the callback patterns observable in the legacy files, effectively overriding the async/await rule
Neither — subdirectory CLAUDE.md files override all parent files when working in that directory
Whichever CLAUDE.md was most recently modified takes precedence
explanation: CLAUDE.md files load hierarchically: user-level (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md) first, then project root, then subdirectories — all active simultaneously. The personal rule 'match the existing code style' instructs Claude to mirror the callback patterns present in the legacy code, which then overrides the async/await directive in practice. Use /memory to see which files are loaded and diagnose which rule is winning.

question: Your team maintains three separate convention files: api-contracts.md, testing-standards.md, and security-policies.md. To make Claude follow them, a developer copied all three into the root CLAUDE.md in full — now it is over 5,000 tokens and any update to one convention requires editing two files. A teammate proposes splitting into three subdirectory CLAUDE.md files. Another suggests moving them to skills. What approach keeps conventions in their own maintainable files while loading them every session without the token duplication?
options: Three subdirectory CLAUDE.md files — this splits content across directories but Claude loads all of them, keeping per-file maintenance while distributing the token cost

Use @import directives in the root CLAUDE.md to reference each file by path — the files remain separate and independently maintainable, updates are picked up automatically, and the root file stays concise
Move all three to skills so they load on demand — this reduces per-turn token cost and keeps files separate
Use {{include}} syntax in CLAUDE.md to inline each file's content at load time
explanation: @import in CLAUDE.md resolves relative file paths at load time, pulling in the referenced file's content. Convention files remain independently maintainable — editing api-contracts.md is picked up automatically without touching CLAUDE.md. This satisfies all the requirements: separate files, no duplication, available every session. Subdirectory CLAUDE.md files (A) only load when Claude is working in that directory. Skills (C) require explicit invocation and don't load every session. {{include}} is not valid CLAUDE.md syntax.

question: A team's root CLAUDE.md is 3,800 tokens. Over an 8-turn bug fix conversation, this means roughly 30,400 tokens are spent on CLAUDE.md injection alone. A teammate argues this is fine because 'Claude needs all that context.' What is the problem with this reasoning, and what should guide what belongs in CLAUDE.md?
options: 30,400 tokens of CLAUDE.md injection is reasonable — context limits are high enough that this is not a practical concern

CLAUDE.md loads on every turn regardless of the current task. Only durable, always-relevant facts (build commands, key conventions, critical gotchas) belong there. Occasional-use content should move to skills or path-scoped rules to avoid the constant token tax
The solution is to compress CLAUDE.md with shorter phrasing while preserving all guidance
Splitting CLAUDE.md into separate files via @import fixes the problem — the token cost stays the same but maintenance is easier
explanation: CLAUDE.md loads on every single turn, making its token count a fixed per-turn tax. Deployment runbooks, occasional-use checklists, and specialised guides not relevant to everyday tasks waste this budget constantly. The right inclusion criterion is: 'would a developer benefit from this on virtually every task?' Skills and path-scoped rules handle the 'sometimes relevant' content without the constant overhead.

question: You ask Claude to review code it just wrote in the same conversation session. Why might this self-review be less effective than a fresh review?
options: Claude refuses to review its own code due to policy restrictions
The same session context that led to the original flawed decisions is still present, biasing the review toward the same blind spots
Self-review uses more tokens than necessary, causing context overflow
Claude loses access to the code after writing it and cannot reference it in the review
explanation: Within the same session, Claude's review is anchored to the same context and implicit assumptions that produced the original code. Starting a fresh session without the generation context produces more independent — and often more critical — feedback.

question: An agent is building a financial report from 200 source documents. It progressively summarises earlier findings to stay within the context window. What specific risk must be managed?
options: Summaries use more tokens than originals, causing rapid context growth
Progressive summarisation tends to drop precise numbers, dates, and named entities in favour of general trends, which can corrupt financial analysis
The agent may loop infinitely creating summaries of summaries
Source documents become inaccessible after summarisation since they are deleted from the session
explanation: Summarisation naturally compresses away specific values — exact figures, dates, account numbers — in favour of narrative. For financial reports where precision is critical, progressive summarisation must be designed to explicitly preserve key data points, or those values should be stored separately.

question: A RAG system retrieves 20 documents and places them all in the context window. Users report that critical information from documents 8-14 is frequently missed in responses. What is the most likely cause and best mitigation?
options: The model refuses to process more than 7 documents; reduce the retrieval count
The 'lost in the middle' effect — models attend less to content in the middle of long contexts; place the most important documents first or last
Documents 8-14 are in a different language; add translation preprocessing
The context window is too small; upgrade to a model with a larger window
explanation: Research confirms a 'lost in the middle' effect: models perform better on information at the beginning or end of long contexts than in the middle. Mitigations include placing the most relevant content first, reranking retrieved documents by relevance, or reducing the number of retrieved documents.

question: A tool returns 50,000 tokens of raw log output, but the agent only needs the error lines. What is the best approach?
options: Pass all 50,000 tokens to the agent and let it filter the relevant parts
Filter and trim the tool output before returning it to the agent, passing only the relevant error lines
Increase the context window size to accommodate the full log output
Ask the agent to call the tool multiple times with smaller time ranges
explanation: Verbose tool output wastes context budget and can push important information out of the window. Tool results should be pre-filtered to return only what the agent needs. This is a key context engineering practice: curate inputs, don't just dump raw data.

question: An agent is performing a complex, multi-hour analysis task. It needs to track intermediate findings, hypotheses, and next steps without losing them to context compaction. What is the recommended practice?
options: Keep all intermediate work in the conversation transcript and rely on the model's memory
Write intermediate findings to scratchpad files on disk, reading them back as needed
Spawn a new sub-agent to memorise the findings for later retrieval
Increase the context window size to hold all intermediate work
explanation: Scratchpad files on disk persist independently of context compaction. They act as external memory the agent can write to and read from, preventing critical intermediate state from being lost when the context window is compressed.

question: An hour into a debugging session, Claude suggests switching to a callback-based approach — directly contradicting the async/await decision you both agreed on 45 minutes ago. You still need Claude's help finishing the current feature and do not want to lose the working context you have built up. You want to prevent further contradictions from early context being dropped, but starting completely over would cost significant time. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Use /reset to wipe the session and start fresh, then re-explain the async/await decision and all prior context

Use /compact to summarise and compress earlier turns, freeing context space while keeping the session alive — then immediately restate the async/await constraint so it is prominent in the remaining context
Open a new session and paste in the current conversation transcript so Claude has full context without compression loss
Continue the session and trust that Claude will self-correct as more context accumulates
explanation: /compact triggers context compaction — it summarises earlier turns to reduce token count while keeping the session and working state alive. After compacting, restating the async/await constraint ensures it is prominent and less likely to be lost again. /reset (A) wipes the session entirely, losing all working context. Pasting the transcript (C) into a new session hits the same context limits. Continuing without action (D) does not address why the contradiction happened.

question: A search tool returns 200 results when the agent only needs the top 5 most relevant matches. What is the best design decision?
options: Return all 200 results so the model has maximum information to work with
Return only the top 5 results from the tool, trimming the response before it enters the context window
Return all 200 results but instruct the model in the system prompt to only read the first 5
Cache the remaining 195 results in a separate file for potential later use
explanation: Tool responses should be sized to what the agent actually needs. Returning 200 results when 5 are needed wastes context budget, increases processing time, and can dilute the model's attention. Pre-filter at the tool layer, not at the prompt layer.

question: Your research agent synthesises findings from 15 sources. The final report makes factual claims but the downstream system can't verify which claims came from which sources. What should the agent architecture include?
options: An appendix listing all 15 sources without linking them to specific claims
Inline citation mapping that links each factual claim to the specific source(s) it came from
A confidence score for the overall report quality
A disclaimer that all claims are approximate and may not reflect source materials
explanation: Inline citation mapping (claim-to-source linkage) enables downstream verification, auditability, and trust. A source list without claim linkage (A) doesn't allow specific claim verification. Confidence scores (C) and disclaimers (D) are workarounds that don't solve the provenance problem.

question: Your research agent finds that two authoritative sources give conflicting statistics for the same metric (one reports 34%, the other 41%). What is the correct agent behaviour?
options: Average the two values and report 37.5% as the consensus figure
Use whichever source was published more recently and discard the other
Report both values, note the discrepancy, attribute each to its source, and flag the conflict for human review
Choose the more conservative (lower) value to avoid overstating findings
explanation: When authoritative sources conflict, the agent should not silently resolve the conflict by choosing or averaging — that hides important uncertainty. The correct behaviour is to transparently surface the discrepancy, attribute each figure to its source, and flag it for human judgement.

question: You're using Claude to generate a complex technical specification, but early drafts miss key requirements. Which iterative pattern is most effective for gathering the right requirements before writing?
options: Provide all requirements in one massive upfront prompt and iterate on the output
Use an interview pattern: ask Claude to pose clarifying questions before drafting, then answer them before generating the spec
Start generating and correct errors manually after each paragraph
Provide a single vague prompt and rely on Claude to infer the requirements from context
explanation: The interview pattern front-loads ambiguity resolution. By having Claude ask clarifying questions first, you surface requirement gaps before any generation happens, leading to a spec that fits your actual needs without multiple costly revision cycles.

question: Which iteration pattern is most effective when generating code that must pass a specific set of tests?
options: Generate code, manually inspect it, and rewrite the prompt if it looks wrong
Generate code, run the tests, feed failing test output back to Claude, and repeat until all tests pass
Provide the tests and ask Claude to generate code that you believe should pass them, then submit without running
Run the tests first, then generate code based on which tests existed before adding new ones
explanation: Test-driven iteration uses actual test results as grounding signal — the model receives concrete, objective feedback rather than subjective judgement. Feeding failing test output closes the loop precisely and prevents hallucinated correctness.

question: Your automated code review flags too many false positives, annoying developers. The system prompt currently says "flag any code quality issues." What change most effectively reduces false positives?
options: Lower the model's temperature to make it more conservative
Replace the vague instruction with explicit, enumerated criteria specifying exactly what should and should not be flagged, including threshold examples
Ask the model to self-rate its confidence and only surface high-confidence findings
Switch to a larger model that better understands code quality
explanation: Vague instructions like 'flag any code quality issues' leave decision boundaries up to the model, producing inconsistent results. Explicit criteria with concrete examples define exactly what counts as a flaggable issue, directly reducing false positives. Confidence self-rating (C) is poorly calibrated.

question: You need Claude to extract structured data (title, author, publication_date, summary) from unstructured articles. Which approach produces the most reliable, parseable output?
options: Ask Claude to 'summarise the article and mention the author and date'
Define a tool with a JSON schema for the fields and use tool_use to force the model to populate the schema
Ask Claude to output a markdown table with the fields as columns
Use a regex to extract fields from Claude's prose response after the fact
explanation: tool_use with a JSON schema forces the model to populate a defined structure, producing validated, consistently formatted output. Prose and markdown output (A, C) require fragile post-processing. Regex on prose (D) is brittle and error-prone.

question: When defining a JSON schema for structured extraction, which field design practice best prevents the model from hallucinating values for missing data?
options: Mark all fields as required so the model always provides a value
Use nullable fields (allowing null values) so the model can explicitly return null when data is absent rather than fabricating a value
Omit optional fields from the schema so the model skips them automatically
Add a string field named 'confidence' so the model flags uncertain extractions
explanation: Nullable fields give the model a legitimate way to express 'this data is absent,' which is preferable to the model fabricating a plausible-sounding value. Required non-nullable fields (A) create pressure to hallucinate. Omitting fields (C) prevents structured capture entirely.

question: Your pipeline validates Claude's JSON output against a schema and retries if validation fails. After 3 retries, the same malformed field keeps appearing. What should the retry loop include to break this pattern?
options: Increase max retries to 10 — more attempts will eventually produce a valid result
Switch to a different model on each retry
Include the specific validation error message in the retry prompt so the model knows exactly what was wrong
Reset to a fresh session and start over without any context about the previous failures
explanation: Retries without new information repeat the same failure. Including the specific validation error tells the model exactly what constraint was violated, giving it the feedback needed to correct the specific issue. Simply retrying more (A) or switching models (B) without feedback won't reliably fix a structural schema compliance issue.

question: Your batch pipeline submits 500 contract documents for clause extraction overnight. Results arrive 8 hours later but are in a completely different order than you submitted them, and the result file contains no document names or file paths — only Claude's extracted data. Your downstream system needs to write each result back to the correct source document. How should the pipeline have been designed to make this matching possible?
options: Submit documents in alphabetical order and assume results arrive in the same order — the Batches API preserves submission sequence
Include the document filename in the prompt text so Claude echoes it back in the response for post-hoc extraction
Assign each request a unique custom_id at submission time — each result in the batch response carries the same custom_id, providing reliable correlation regardless of result ordering
Use the result timestamps to reconstruct the original submission order by sorting chronologically
explanation: The Message Batches API does not guarantee result ordering — results can arrive in any sequence. The designed solution is custom_id: each request gets a unique identifier at submission (e.g., the document filename or database ID), and each result in the response carries that same custom_id for correlation. Positional indexing (A) breaks because ordering is not preserved. Embedding filenames in prompts (B) requires fragile text parsing. Timestamps (D) do not reflect submission order.

question: An engineering manager proposes using the Message Batches API for two new workflows to cut API costs by 50%: (1) a nightly job that scores 20,000 customer support tickets for priority and routes them to queues before the morning shift, and (2) a live support chat feature where customers expect responses within 3 seconds and the conversation can span multiple back-and-forth turns. Which proposal is sound?
options: Both are sound — the Batches API's 50% cost reduction applies to any volume workload and the response time is configurable

Only workflow (1) — the Batches API processes requests asynchronously with no latency SLA (up to 24 hours) and does not support multi-turn conversations. Workflow (2) requires synchronous real-time API calls.
Only workflow (2) — the Batches API is designed for interactive workloads that need guaranteed throughput, not overnight batch jobs
Both would work but workflow (2) needs to be redesigned to use polling instead of a persistent connection
explanation: The Batches API is designed for high-volume, latency-tolerant, single-turn requests — overnight scoring of 20,000 tickets (workflow 1) is an ideal fit. Workflow (2) fails on two counts: the API provides no latency SLA (could take up to 24 hours, not 3 seconds) and does not support multi-turn conversations where each response depends on the previous one. Live chat requires the synchronous API.

question: A developer asks Claude to 'improve the performance of this function' without providing any other context. What is the ideal Claude behaviour before making changes?
options: Make the most common performance optimisations immediately to save time
Ask clarifying questions: What performance metric matters? What are the constraints? Is correctness more important than speed?
Refuse the task until a complete requirements document is provided
Add logging to measure current performance, then optimise based on the measurements
explanation: Ambiguous tasks should trigger clarifying questions before action. 'Improve performance' could mean latency, throughput, memory, or readability — and tradeoffs differ. Asking first prevents wasted effort on the wrong optimisation.

question: When is asking Claude to 'think step by step' most beneficial?
options: For simple lookup tasks where the answer is a single known fact
For multi-step reasoning tasks where intermediate steps are needed to reach the correct conclusion
Only for maths problems — it has no effect on other task types
When you want shorter, more concise responses
explanation: Chain-of-thought prompting helps most on tasks requiring multi-step reasoning — complex analysis, logic puzzles, planning, or inference chains. For simple factual lookups, it adds tokens without improving accuracy. It generally produces longer responses, not shorter ones.

question: Your API uses Claude for three different use cases: (1) generating legal contract language that must be precise and conservative, (2) writing marketing copy that should be creative and engaging, and (3) providing customer support responses that should be warm and empathetic. All three share the same generic system prompt and produce responses that feel inconsistently toned. What is the most effective fix?
options: Use a different Claude model for each use case — some models specialise in legal, creative, or empathetic tasks

Assign a domain-specific role in each system prompt ('You are a senior contracts attorney...', 'You are an award-winning copywriter...', 'You are an empathetic support specialist...') to activate the relevant vocabulary, tone, and reasoning patterns
Add explicit tone adjectives to each user message rather than the system prompt so they can vary per request
Fine-tune separate model versions for each use case
explanation: Role assignment in the system prompt primes the model with domain-specific vocabulary, risk tolerance, and tone that persist across the entire session. Per-message tone hints (C) work but require repetition on every call and are less consistent. Model specialisation (A, D) is unnecessary overhead when prompt-level role assignment handles the problem directly.

question: Your code review agent produces wildly inconsistent feedback between runs — sometimes focusing on style, sometimes security, sometimes performance — with no predictable pattern. All review criteria are embedded in the user message alongside the diff. What architectural change most improves consistency?
options: Set the model's temperature to 0 to eliminate randomness
Move the review criteria, focus areas, output format, and agent role into the system prompt, which is injected consistently on every run. User messages then carry only the diff
Run each PR through 5 independent passes and only report issues that appear in at least 3 of 5
Ask the agent to self-rate confidence on each finding and surface only findings above 0.8
explanation: The system prompt establishes the agent's persistent configuration: role, focus areas, output format, and behavioural rules. When review criteria live in user messages, they are one-off instructions that shift between runs. Moving them to the system prompt ensures every review starts from the same baseline regardless of what the user message contains.
